posts.text:
De la Espriella's victory in Colombia could accelerate energy projects, but major hurdles remain

▪️De la Espriella win may restart oil exploration
▪️Constitutional limits block fracking, licensing
▪️Gas supply critical as reserve margins tighten

🖥️Full story: https://t.co/Qv7xL0LnB6
posts.summary: De la Espriella's election victory in Colombia could potentially restart oil exploration activities, though constitutional restrictions on fracking and licensing will significantly limit the scope of energy project development. As gas supply becomes increasingly critical amid tightening reserve margins, Colombia faces structural constraints that may prevent a major acceleration of upstream projects despite the political shift.

posts.text: The Strait of Hormuz is no longer simply “open” or “closed.” Analysts warn that GPS spoofing, insurance hurdles, sanctions compliance, and new Iranian transit rules are creating major risks for physical oil flows—risks largely ignored by futures markets. #Oil #Hormuz
posts.summary: Multiple operational and regulatory challenges—including GPS spoofing, insurance constraints, sanctions compliance, and new Iranian transit rules—are creating understated risks to oil shipments through the Strait of Hormuz that futures markets have not adequately priced in. The strait's functionality now depends on factors beyond simple open/closed status, potentially affecting global physical oil flows.

posts.text: Hydrogen’s credibility may be recovering. Germany has committed €1.3 billion to three Danish green hydrogen projects, helping unlock production, demand, and infrastructure simultaneously. The deal could become Europe’s first functioning cross-border hydrogen market. #Hydrogen

The development underscores a strategic shift toward hydrogen infrastructure that could compete with conventional energy markets.

posts.text: Non-oil export revenues totaled $2.27 billion in the first quarter, 15.5% more than in the same period of 2025, according to the central bank’s data. Total crude export revenues last year were $17.1 billion, according to the bank.
posts.summary: Venezuela's non-oil exports grew 15.5% year-over-year in the first quarter, while crude oil revenues totaled $17.1 billion for the previous year, indicating the country remains heavily dependent on oil sales despite modest diversification gains.

posts.text: Venezuela has seen a rebound this year in its crude production and exports to the United States and Europe after Washington eased sanctions and foreign companies expanded oil and gas projects after the U.S. ouster of President Nicolas Maduro in January.
posts.summary: Venezuela's crude production and exports have increased in 2025 following U.S. sanctions relief and the removal of President Nicolas Maduro, with foreign companies resuming oil and gas operations. This rebound affects global oil supplies, particularly for markets in the United States and Europe.

posts.text: Climate resilience is becoming a core investment priority as weather-related losses mount worldwide. London is positioning itself as the leading hub for green finance, clean energy investment, and carbon markets, arguing climate action is now an economic necessity. #NetZero
posts.summary: Climate resilience is becoming a major investment priority globally, with London establishing itself as a hub for green finance and clean energy investment, signaling a structural shift toward climate-focused capital allocation. This trend reflects growing recognition that climate action represents an economic necessity rather than merely an environmental imperative.

posts.text:

https://t.co/XuJ9JPeESa

This reallocation of drilling resources suggests continued focus on oil development in Western Canada.

posts.text: More than €23 billion ($26.3B) has flowed into Greece’s energy sector in just one month as investors position for Europe’s shift away from Russian gas. Massive investments in power grids, renewables, and infrastructure are strengthening Greece’s role as a regional energy hub.
posts.summary: Greece is attracting substantial investment in energy infrastructure and renewables as Europe reduces dependence on Russian gas, positioning the country as a regional energy hub. The €23 billion inflow suggests a shift in European energy markets away from fossil fuel imports toward alternative infrastructure and renewable capacity.

posts.text: A U.S.-Iran MOU may have eased fears temporarily, but Oilprice warns the real threat is dwindling global oil inventories. As “tank bottoms” approach and Hormuz traffic remains restricted, a supply crunch could trigger a new surge in crude prices. #Oil #Iran #Hormuz #EnergyMarkets
posts.summary: Global oil inventories are declining toward critical levels while shipping restrictions in the Strait of Hormuz persist, creating conditions that could trigger a significant increase in crude oil prices despite recent diplomatic easing between the U.S. and Iran.

posts.text: Tough week to project US gasoline demand. Solstice week 2025 saw 9.688-mil b/d, which was easily the highest weekly number of the year. In 2022, $5/gallon gas led to a miserable figure of 8.5-mil b/d. Difference of 1.1-mil b/d is more than most countries consume.
posts.summary: US gasoline demand reached 9.688 million barrels per day during the winter solstice week of 2025, marking the year's highest weekly consumption and significantly exceeding demand levels seen during the 2022 price spike, suggesting robust fuel consumption despite higher prices.

posts.text:

posts.summary: The White House's waiver allowing US purchases of Iranian oil appears unlikely to have immediate market impact, as US refiners are improbable to buy Iranian crude in the near term, raising questions about whether the move was primarily symbolic diplomacy toward Iran rather than a substantive policy shift.

posts.text: US put ~9.1 million barrels of SPR oil into the market last week, the 2nd largest weekly release ever (equal to ~1.3 million b/d). Over the last 8 weeks, the US has been releasing >1m b/d on average.
posts.summary: The US released approximately 9.1 million barrels from its Strategic Petroleum Reserve last week at a rate of 1.3 million barrels per day, marking the second-largest weekly release on record, with sustained releases averaging over 1 million barrels per day over the past two months. This substantial injection of supply into the market represents a significant intervention in global oil availability.

posts.text: Perhaps more important than the 9.1-million barrel drop in the SPR last week, the DOE awarded only 500,000 barrels in a sales process for 40-million barrels of crude. Sole accepted bid was Vitol. Market structure no longer supports replacing oil with swollen volumes.
posts.summary: The DOE's Strategic Petroleum Reserve sale attracted minimal bidding, with only Vitol accepting 500,000 of the 40-million barrels offered, suggesting weak market demand for large crude volumes at current prices. This indicates structural market conditions may no longer support the high oil supply levels needed to replenish the SPR.

posts.text:
What are the key forces shaping the future of global energy and oil markets?

In this latest article, HE Haitham Al Ghais, #OPEC Secretary General, outlines six pivotal trends from the World Oil Outlook 2026, including energy security, rising global demand, investment needs,
posts.summary: OPEC's Secretary General identifies six pivotal trends shaping global energy and oil markets, emphasizing energy security, rising global demand, and investment needs as key forces outlined in the World Oil Outlook 2026. posts.text: BREAKING: U.S. SPR drops 9 million barrels to 331.2 million, 208.2mb of sour, 123.0mb of sweet crude, lowest level since July, 1983.
posts.summary: The U.S. Strategic Petroleum Reserve has fallen to its lowest level since July 1983, dropping 9 million barrels to 331.2 million barrels total, which could limit the government's ability to respond to future oil supply disruptions.

posts.text: Twelve’s AirPlant One, which it bills as the first commercial-scale facility in the U.S. to produce sustainable aviation fuel (SAF), has begun operations in Moses Lake, WA, the company said June 10. Referred to by Twelve as E-Jet fuel, it is an alternative to traditional jet fuel
posts.summary: Twelve's AirPlant One facility in Moses Lake, Washington has begun producing sustainable aviation fuel (SAF) at commercial scale, marking the first such U.S. facility and potentially offering an alternative to traditional jet fuel. The development could impact oil demand if SAF adoption accelerates in the aviation sector.

posts.text: So US companies can now buy Iranian crude. There were some piecemeal sales of a few barrels from 2021-2023 but the last significant years for Iranian imports were 1979 (297k/day); 1978 (554k) and 1977 (530k). Guessing the importers were wearing bell-bottoms.
posts.summary: US companies can now purchase Iranian crude oil, marking a significant development after decades of minimal imports since the 1979 Iranian Revolution. The last substantial Iranian oil imports to the US occurred in the late 1970s, when daily volumes exceeded 500,000 barrels.

posts.text: US avg. retail diesel now $5.013/gal with sharply lower numbers soon. But average street prices can be 50cts/gal to $1/gal higher than what large fleets pay, since most buy their fuel on a cost-plus basis. Retail diesel averages are among the most misleading bench marks.
posts.summary: US retail diesel averaged $5.013 per gallon with expectations for further declines, though retail prices typically run 50 cents to $1 per gallon higher than fleet wholesale rates due to cost-plus purchasing agreements, making retail averages an unreliable benchmark for actual market conditions.

posts.text:
It may only last 60 days (I doubt it; likely would become permanent), but the US Treasury’s waiver is effectively rolling back 40-year plus of American oil sanctions against Iran. 

Even US refiners are allowed to import Iranian oil and pay for it in greenback!!! https://t.co/nG7xDt1Fwj
posts.summary: The U.S. Treasury has issued a waiver that allows American refiners to import Iranian oil and pay in dollars, effectively reversing decades of sanctions against Iran's oil sector. The move could have significant implications for global oil markets, though it is currently set to last 60 days.

posts.text:
Heatwaves can put major strain on energy systems as demand for cooling sends power use soaring.
 
In this 2025 podcast, IEA’s Brian Motherway looks at the rapid growth in air conditioning demand globally – and how to keep cool without overloading grids 👉 https://t.co/mDJZ2DMM9d https://t.co/Qcj5sHh4dL
posts.summary: Rising global air conditioning demand during heatwaves increases electricity consumption, which can strain power grids and potentially boost demand for energy sources used in power generation. The challenge of meeting cooling needs efficiently has implications for energy systems and resource allocation across the sector.

posts.text:
Crucially, the general license not only allows importers of Iranian oil to pay Tehran (including via previously sanctioned entities), **but also do so in U.S. dollars!**

For 60 days at least, Washington is allowing Tehran back in the dollar club to repatriate its petrodollars.
posts.summary: The U.S. has issued a general license permitting importers of Iranian oil to pay Tehran directly in U.S. dollars for at least 60 days, effectively reintegrating Iran into dollar-denominated oil trade and allowing the country to accumulate petrodollars. This policy shift could facilitate increased Iranian oil exports by removing payment barriers that sanctions had previously created.

posts.text:
Another important caveat to these exit flows.

While loadings have indeed begun to rise, led above all by the UAE (master of the shadow transits), those loadings are still running at less than half the pace of these boosted exits.

Inherently unsustainable without more ships in. https://t.co/UvYM6gr9Js
posts.summary: Oil loadings are rising but remain insufficient to match the elevated exit flows from storage, creating an unsustainable situation that would require additional tanker capacity to balance. This supply-demand mismatch in crude transport logistics could have implications for oil price stability and inventory management.
